𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Eleven — Part One

The Book of Mishlei

MISHLEI

The Proverbs of Shlomo

Chapters 1 through 10

The Fear of YHWH — Wisdom Discourses — The Proverbs Begin

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

No complete DSS manuscript for Mishlei — wisdom tradition preserved in Orit Ge'ez

Mishlei wisdom theology foundational to 1QS Community Rule and Hodayot at Qumran

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Mishlei preserved in full

Masoretic Text — primary manuscript witness for Mishlei

YHWH and The Creator only — no substitute titles

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MISHLEI — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 10

THE WISDOM DISCOURSES — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 9

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 1 The Beginning of Wisdom — Fear of YHWH — A Father's Instruction

1 The proverbs of Shlomo, son of Dawid, king of Yisra'EL:

2 To know wisdom and instruction, to understand words of insight,

3 To receive instruction in wise dealing, in righteousness, justice, and equity;

4 To give prudence to the simple, knowledge and discretion to the youth —

5 Let the wise hear and increase in learning, and the one who understands obtain guidance,

6 To understand a proverb and a saying, the words of the wise and their riddles.

7 The fear of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge — fools despise wisdom and instruction.

8 Hear, my son, your father's instruction, and forsake not your mother's teaching,

9 For they are a graceful garland for your head and pendants for your neck.

10 My son, if sinners entice you, do not consent.

11 If they say, Come with us, let us lie in wait for blood — let us ambush the innocent without reason —

12 Like Sheol let us swallow them alive, and whole, like those who go down to the pit.

13 We shall find all precious goods — we shall fill our houses with plunder.

14 Throw in your lot among us — we will all have one purse —

15 My son, do not walk in the way with them — hold back your foot from their paths,

16 For their feet run to evil, and they make haste to shed blood.

17 For in vain is a net spread in the sight of any bird,

18 But these men lie in wait for their own blood — they set an ambush for their own lives.

19 Such are the ways of everyone who is greedy for unjust gain — it takes away the life of its possessors.

20 Wisdom cries aloud in the street — in the markets she raises her voice.

21 At the head of the noisy streets she cries out — at the entrance of the city gates she speaks:

22 How long, O simple ones, will you love being simple? How long will scoffers delight in their scoffing and fools hate knowledge?

23 If you turn at my reproof, behold, I will pour out my spirit to you — I will make my words known to you.

24 Because I have called and you refused to listen, have stretched out my hand and no one has heeded,

25 Because you have ignored all my counsel and would have none of my reproof,

26 I also will laugh at your calamity — I will mock when terror strikes you,

27 When terror strikes you like a storm and your calamity comes like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish come upon you.

28 Then they will call upon me, but I will not answer — they will seek me diligently but will not find me.

29 Because they hated knowledge and did not choose the fear of YHWH,

30 Would have none of my counsel and despised all my reproof,

31 Therefore they shall eat the fruit of their way, and have their fill of their own devices.

32 For the simple are killed by their turning away, and the complacency of fools destroys them.

33 But whoever listens to me will dwell secure and will be at ease, without dread of disaster.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Mishlei has no direct DSS attestation as a complete manuscript. No Cave scrolls preserve large portions of Mishlei. The book's wisdom tradition predates the Qumran community but the wisdom theology of Mishlei — particularly the identification of Wisdom with Torah and the fear of YHWH as the beginning of knowledge — is foundational to the Community Rule and the Hodayot found at Qumran.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Mishlei in full as part of the canonical books of the Beta Israel community. The Book of Mishlei is read as the practical covenant wisdom tradition — the lived expression of Torah in daily relationships, speech, work, and character.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant DSS manuscript variants for Mishlei. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. The book is divided in the Masoretic tradition into several collections: the discourses of Chapters 1-9, the proverbs of Shlomo in Chapters 10-22:16, the words of the wise in 22:17-24:34, further proverbs of Shlomo copied by Khizqiyahu's scribes in Chapters 25-29, the words of Agur in Chapter 30, and the words of Lemuel in Chapter 31.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — the fear of YHWH is the beginning of knowledge — fools despise wisdom and instruction. This is the foundational axiom of the entire book — and of the wisdom tradition in the Hebrew canon. The same declaration appears in Psalm 111:10 and in Iyov 28:28. Fear of YHWH is not terror but covenant reverence — the orientation of the whole person toward YHWH as the source and standard of all knowledge. Chapter 1's personified Wisdom crying in the streets — verses 20-21 — is the same figure of Psalm 154 from the DSS. Wisdom is not abstract: she calls out publicly, at the city gate, in the marketplace. The refusal to hear her is not intellectual skepticism — it is the moral choice described in verse 29: they hated knowledge and did not choose the fear of YHWH.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 2 Seek Wisdom Like Silver — She Protects and Preserves the Way

1 My son, if you receive my words and treasure up my commandments with you,

2 Making your ear attentive to wisdom and inclining your heart to understanding,

3 Yes, if you call out for insight and raise your voice for understanding,

4 If you seek it like silver and search for it as for hidden treasures,

5 Then you will understand the fear of YHWH and find the knowledge of Creator.

6 For YHWH gives wisdom — from his mouth come knowledge and understanding.

7 He stores up sound wisdom for the upright — he is a shield to those who walk in integrity,

8 Guarding the paths of justice and watching over the way of his saints.

9 Then you will understand righteousness and justice and equity, every good path.

10 For wisdom will come into your heart, and knowledge will be pleasant to your soul.

11 Discretion will watch over you — understanding will guard you,

12 Delivering you from the way of evil, from men of perverted speech,

13 Who forsake the paths of uprightness to walk in the ways of darkness,

14 Who rejoice in doing evil and delight in the perverseness of evil,

15 Men whose paths are crooked, and who are devious in their ways.

16 So you will be delivered from the forbidden woman, from the adulteress with her smooth words,

17 Who forsakes the companion of her youth and forgets the covenant of her Creator.

18 For her house sinks down to death, and her paths to the departed.

19 None who go to her come back, nor do they regain the paths of life.

20 So you will walk in the way of the good and keep to the paths of the righteous.

21 For the upright will inhabit the land, and those with integrity will remain in it,

22 But the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the treacherous will be rooted out of it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 2. The wisdom tradition preserved here — the careful seeker who treats wisdom as treasure, who receives it as YHWH's gift — is reflected in the Hodayot (Thanksgiving Hymns) from Qumran, where the community describes receiving divine understanding as YHWH's gracious act.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. The identification of wisdom with the gift of YHWH — verse 6 — is central to the Beta Israel theological tradition: human wisdom is not self-generated but received from the mouth of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 2. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 17 — who forsakes the companion of her youth and forgets the covenant of her Creator — is one of the few places in Mishlei where covenant language appears explicitly in the wisdom instruction.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4-5 — if you seek it like silver and search for it as for hidden treasures — then you will understand the fear of YHWH. Wisdom does not come passively. The conditions for receiving it are active: receive, treasure, make the ear attentive, incline the heart, call out, raise the voice, seek, search. All eight verbs are human acts of effort. Yet verse 6 declares: YHWH gives wisdom — from his mouth come knowledge and understanding. The human effort and the divine gift are not contradictory — the effort is the posture of receptivity that positions the seeker to receive what only YHWH can give.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 3 Trust in YHWH With All Your Heart — Do Not Lean on Your Own Understanding

1 My son, do not forget my teaching, but let your heart keep my commandments,

2 For length of days and years of life and peace they will add to you.

3 Let not steadfast love and faithfulness forsake you — bind them around your neck — write them on the tablet of your heart.

4 So you will find favor and good success in the sight of Creator and man.

5 Trust in YHWH with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.

6 In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths.

7 Be not wise in your own eyes — fear YHWH, and turn away from evil.

8 It will be healing to your flesh and refreshment to your bones.

9 Honor YHWH with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your produce.

10 Then your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will be bursting with wine.

11 My son, do not despise YHWH's discipline or be weary of his reproof,

12 For YHWH reproves him whom he loves, as a father the son in whom he delights.

13 Blessed is the one who finds wisdom, and the one who gets understanding,

14 For the gain from her is better than gain from silver and her profit better than gold.

15 She is more precious than jewels, and nothing you desire can compare with her.

16 Long life is in her right hand — in her left hand are riches and honor.

17 Her ways are ways of pleasantness, and all her paths are peace.

18 She is a tree of life to those who lay hold of her — those who hold her fast are called blessed.

19 YHWH by wisdom founded the earth — by understanding he established the heavens.

20 By his knowledge the deeps broke open, and the clouds drop down the dew.

21 My son, do not lose sight of these — keep sound wisdom and discretion,

22 And they will be life for your soul and adornment for your neck.

23 Then you will walk on your way securely, and your foot will not stumble.

24 If you lie down, you will not be afraid — when you lie down, your sleep will be sweet.

25 Do not be afraid of sudden terror or of the ruin of the wicked, when it comes,

26 For YHWH will be your confidence and will keep your foot from being caught.

27 Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due, when it is in your power to do it.

28 Do not say to your neighbor, Go, and come again, tomorrow I will give it — when you have it with you.

29 Do not plan evil against your neighbor, who dwells trustingly beside you.

30 Do not contend with a man for no reason, when he has done you no harm.

31 Do not envy a man of violence and do not choose any of his ways,

32 For the devious person is an abomination to YHWH, but the upright are in his confidence.

33 YHWH's curse is on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the dwelling of the righteous.

34 Toward the scorners he is scornful, but to the humble he gives favor.

35 The wise will inherit honor, but fools get disgrace.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 3. Verse 18 — she is a tree of life to those who lay hold of her — is one of the most significant verses in the wisdom tradition, connecting Wisdom directly to the tree of life in Bereshit 2-3. The DSS community's identification of Torah with the tree of life draws on this passage.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 5-6 — trust in YHWH with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding — in all your ways acknowledge him and he will make straight your paths — is one of the most cited covenant wisdom declarations in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 3. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 12 — YHWH reproves him whom he loves, as a father the son in whom he delights — appears also in the Book of Iyov as the ground of YHWH's discipline of the righteous.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-6 — trust in YHWH with all your heart and do not lean on your own understanding — in all your ways acknowledge him and he will make straight your paths. The covenant principle of Devarim 6:5 — love YHWH your Creator with all your heart — is here expressed as trust. Leaning on one's own understanding is the opposite of covenant trust: self-sufficiency replacing dependence on YHWH. Verse 19 — YHWH by wisdom founded the earth. Wisdom is not merely human practical knowledge — it is the principle by which YHWH created. Wisdom was present at creation before the covenant community existed. The fear of YHWH that leads to wisdom participates in the same principle by which the world was made.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 4 Get Wisdom — Guard Your Heart — The Path of the Righteous Is Like the Dawning Light

1 Hear, O sons, a father's instruction, and be attentive, that you may gain insight,

2 For I give you good precepts — do not forsake my teaching.

3 When I was a son with my father, tender, the only one in the sight of my mother,

4 He taught me and said to me, Let your heart hold fast my words — keep my commandments, and live.

5 Get wisdom — get insight — do not forget, and do not turn away from the words of my mouth.

6 Do not forsake her, and she will keep you — love her, and she will guard you.

7 The beginning of wisdom is this: Get wisdom, and whatever you get, get insight.

8 Prize her highly, and she will exalt you — she will honor you if you embrace her.

9 She will place on your head a graceful garland — she will bestow on you a beautiful crown.

10 Hear, my son, and accept my words, that the years of your life may be many.

11 I have taught you the way of wisdom — I have led you in the paths of uprightness.

12 When you walk, your step will not be hampered, and if you run, you will not stumble.

13 Keep hold of instruction — do not let go — guard her, for she is your life.

14 Do not enter the path of the wicked, and do not walk in the way of the evil.

15 Avoid it — do not go on it — turn away from it and pass on.

16 For they cannot sleep unless they have done wrong — they are robbed of sleep unless they have made someone stumble.

17 For they eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence.

18 But the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, which shines brighter and brighter until full day.

19 The way of the wicked is like deep darkness — they do not know over what they stumble.

20 My son, be attentive to my words — incline your ear to my sayings.

21 Let them not escape from your sight — keep them within your heart.

22 For they are life to those who find them, and healing to all their flesh.

23 Keep your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life.

24 Put away from you crooked speech, and put devious talk far from you.

25 Let your eyes look directly forward, and your gaze be straight before you.

26 Ponder the path of your feet — then all your ways will be sure.

27 Do not swerve to the right or to the left — turn your foot away from evil.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 4. The transmission of wisdom from father to son — the covenant pedagogy of verses 1-9 — mirrors the covenant transmission commanded in Devarim 6:6-7 and reflected throughout the Tehillim.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. The image of the path of the righteous as the light of dawn growing brighter — verse 18 — is read as the covenant person's progressive growth in wisdom throughout a life of faithful Torah observance.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 4. The Masoretic text is the primary witness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18-19 — the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, which shines brighter and brighter until full day. The way of the wicked is like deep darkness — they do not know over what they stumble. The contrast is not merely moral but perceptual: the righteous person sees more clearly over time — the path becomes brighter. The wicked person stumbles in darkness without understanding why. Verse 23 — keep your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life. The heart in the Hebrew wisdom tradition is not primarily the seat of emotion but of will, intention, and character. What flows from the heart — word, deed, decision — determines the shape of the entire life. The vigilance commanded is not anxious self-scrutiny but the covenant care of the interior life.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 5 The Strange Woman — Drink Water From Your Own Cistern — Rejoice in the Wife of Your Youth

1 My son, be attentive to my wisdom — incline your ear to my understanding,

2 That you may keep discretion, and your lips may guard knowledge.

3 For the lips of a forbidden woman drip honey, and her speech is smoother than oil,

4 But in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a two-edged sword.

5 Her feet go down to death — her steps follow the path to Sheol.

6 She does not ponder the path of life — her ways wander, and she does not know it.

7 And now, O sons, listen to me, and do not depart from the words of my mouth.

8 Keep your way far from her, and do not go near the door of her house,

9 Lest you give your honor to others and your years to the merciless,

10 Lest strangers take their fill of your strength, and your labors go to the house of a foreigner,

11 And at the end of your life you groan, when your flesh and body are consumed,

12 And you say, How I hated discipline, and my heart despised reproof.

13 I did not listen to the voice of my teachers or incline my ear to my instructors.

14 I am at the brink of utter ruin in the assembled congregation.

15 Drink water from your own cistern, flowing water from your own well.

16 Should your springs be scattered abroad, streams of water in the streets?

17 Let them be for yourself alone, and not for strangers with you.

18 Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth,

19 A lovely deer, a graceful doe. Let her breasts fill you at all times with delight — be intoxicated always in her love.

20 Why should you be intoxicated, my son, with a forbidden woman and embrace the bosom of an adulteress?

21 For a man's ways are before the eyes of YHWH, and he ponders all his paths.

22 The iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin.

23 He dies for lack of discipline, and because of his great folly he is led astray.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 5. The covenant warning against the forbidden woman — whose feet go down to death — is consistent with the DSS community's strict regulations around sexual purity, which are detailed in the Damascus Document and the Community Rule.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. The positive instruction of verses 15-19 — rejoice in the wife of your youth, be intoxicated in her love — is read as the covenant affirmation of covenant marriage as the proper context for sexual delight.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 5. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 21 — a man's ways are before the eyes of YHWH, and he ponders all his paths — is the theological anchor of the entire sexual ethics instruction: YHWH sees all paths, and no path is hidden from him.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — the lips of a forbidden woman drip honey, and her speech is smoother than oil, but in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a two-edged sword. Mishlei's wisdom consistently alerts the covenant person to the gap between appearance and reality. What appears sweet — the honey and oil of the forbidden woman's speech — is in the end lethal. Verse 21 — a man's ways are before the eyes of YHWH. The same omniscience declared in Psalm 139:1-3. No path, however private, is hidden. The covenant wisdom of Mishlei does not call for abstinence from pleasure — verse 18 commands rejoicing in the wife of one's youth. The warning is against the counterfeit that destroys while appearing to satisfy.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 6 The Sluggard — Six Things YHWH Hates — Seven Abominations

1 My son, if you have put up security for your neighbor, have given your pledge for a stranger,

2 If you are snared in the words of your mouth, caught in the words of your mouth,

3 Then do this, my son, and save yourself, for you have come into the hand of your neighbor: go, hasten, and plead urgently with your neighbor.

4 Give your eyes no sleep and your eyelids no slumber.

5 Save yourself like a gazelle from the hand of the hunter, like a bird from the hand of the fowler.

6 Go to the ant, O sluggard — consider her ways, and be wise.

7 Without having any chief, officer, or ruler,

8 She prepares her bread in summer and gathers her food in harvest.

9 How long will you lie there, O sluggard? When will you arise from your sleep?

10 A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,

11 And poverty will come upon you like a robber, and want like an armed man.

12 A worthless person, a wicked man, goes about with crooked speech,

13 Winks with his eyes, signals with his feet, points with his finger,

14 With perverted heart devises evil, continually sowing discord.

15 Therefore calamity will come upon him suddenly — in a moment he will be broken beyond healing.

16 There are six things that YHWH hates, seven that are an abomination to him:

17 Haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood,

18 A heart that devises wicked plans, feet that make haste to run to evil,

19 A false witness who breathes out lies, and one who sows discord among brothers.

20 My son, keep your father's commandment, and forsake not your mother's teaching.

21 Bind them on your heart always — tie them around your neck.

22 When you walk, they will lead you — when you lie down, they will watch over you — and when you awake, they will talk with you.

23 For the commandment is a lamp and the teaching a light, and the reproofs of discipline are the way of life,

24 To preserve you from the evil woman, from the smooth tongue of the adulteress.

25 Do not desire her beauty in your heart, and do not let her capture you with her eyelashes.

26 For the price of a prostitute is only a loaf of bread, but a married woman hunts down a precious life.

27 Can a man carry fire next to his chest and his clothes not be burned?

28 Or can one walk on hot coals and his feet not be scorched?

29 So is he who goes in to his neighbor's wife — none who touches her will go unpunished.

30 People do not despise a thief if he steals to satisfy his appetite when he is hungry.

31 But if he is caught, he will pay sevenfold — he will give all the goods of his house.

32 He who commits adultery lacks sense — he who does it destroys himself.

33 For jealousy makes a man furious, and he will not spare when he takes revenge.

34 He will accept no compensation — he will refuse though you multiply gifts.

35 He will accept no compensation — he will refuse though you multiply gifts.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 6. The list of seven abominations in verses 16-19 — haughty eyes, lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that run to evil, a false witness, one who sows discord — is a complete catalog of the behaviors the Qumran community's Community Rule identifies as violations requiring disciplinary action.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. The commandment as lamp and teaching as light — verse 23 — is read as the covenant declaration that Torah illuminates the path of life and exposes the path of death.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] Verse 35 is identical to verse 34 in some manuscripts, reflecting a possible scribal repetition. This compilation follows the Masoretic tradition of presenting verse 35 as a distinct verse emphasizing the absolute refusal of compensation in cases of adultery. No other significant variants.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 16-19 — six things YHWH hates, seven that are an abomination to him. The numerical structure — six... seven — is the Hebrew literary device of climactic enumeration found also in Amos 1-2. The seven abominations are not random but form a coherent portrait of the destructive person: pride in the eyes, deception in speech, violence in the hands, evil in the heart, urgency in running to wrong, lying in testimony, and division among the covenant community. Verse 19 — one who sows discord among brothers — is the climax: the person who breaks community is placed in the same catalog as murderers and liars. Covenant community is sacred. Its destruction is an abomination to YHWH.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 7 The Seductive Woman — Her House Is the Way to Sheol

1 My son, keep my words and treasure up my commandments with you.

2 Keep my commandments and live — keep my teaching as the apple of your eye.

3 Bind them on your fingers — write them on the tablet of your heart.

4 Say to wisdom, You are my sister, and call insight your intimate friend,

5 To keep you from the forbidden woman, from the adulteress with her smooth words.

6 For at the window of my house I have looked out through my lattice,

7 And I have seen among the simple, I have perceived among the youths, a young man lacking sense,

8 Passing along the street near her corner, taking the road to her house

9 In the twilight, in the evening, at the time of night and darkness.

10 And behold, the woman meets him, dressed as a prostitute, wily of heart.

11 She is loud and wayward — her feet do not stay at home.

12 Now in the street, now in the markets, and at every corner she lies in wait.

13 She seizes him and kisses him, and with bold face she says to him,

14 I had to offer sacrifices, and today I have paid my vows.

15 So now I have come out to meet you, to seek you eagerly, and I have found you.

16 I have spread my couch with coverings, colored linens from Egyptian linen.

17 I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.

18 Come, let us take our fill of love till morning — let us delight ourselves with love.

19 For my husband is not at home — he has gone on a long journey.

20 He took a bag of money with him — at full moon he will come home.

21 With much seductive speech she persuades him — with her smooth talk she compels him.

22 All at once he follows her, as an ox goes to the slaughter, or as a stag is caught fast

23 Till an arrow pierces its liver — as a bird rushes into a snare — he does not know that it will cost him his life.

24 And now, O sons, listen to me, and be attentive to the words of my mouth.

25 Let not your heart turn aside to her ways — do not stray into her paths,

26 For many a victim has she laid low, and all her slain are a mighty throng.

27 Her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 7. The extended narrative of the young man lacking sense who follows the seductive woman to his death is one of the most vivid cautionary narratives in the wisdom literature. The DSS community's regulations against sexual compromise reflect the same covenant concern.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. The closing declaration — her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death — verse 27 — is read as the definitive covenant warning: the path of sexual unfaithfulness leads not merely to personal ruin but to the realm of death.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 7. The Masoretic text is the primary witness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22-23 — all at once he follows her, as an ox goes to the slaughter — as a stag caught fast — till an arrow pierces its liver — as a bird rushes into a snare — he does not know that it will cost him his life. Three animal images in quick succession: ox, stag, bird. Each moves willingly into its destruction. The ox follows the drover to slaughter. The stag runs into the trap. The bird flies into the snare. None of them is violent or forced. The death is the result of not knowing — he does not know. The wisdom tradition's fundamental concern with knowledge — da'at — is here its negative: ignorance of consequence is itself fatal. Verse 4 — say to wisdom, you are my sister. The antidote to the seductive woman is not suppression of desire but the covenant relationship with Wisdom personified as family — sister, intimate friend.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 8 Wisdom Cries Out — I Was Beside Him at Creation — Whoever Finds Me Finds Life

1 Does not wisdom call? Does not understanding raise her voice?

2 On the heights beside the way, at the crossroads she takes her stand.

3 Beside the gates in front of the town, at the entrance of the portals she cries aloud:

4 To you, O men, I call, and my cry is to the children of man.

5 O simple ones, learn prudence — O fools, learn sense.

6 Hear, for I will speak noble things, and from my lips will come what is right.

7 For my mouth will utter truth — wickedness is an abomination to my lips.

8 All the words of my mouth are righteous — there is nothing twisted or crooked in them.

9 They are all straight to him who understands, and right to those who find knowledge.

10 Take my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold.

11 For wisdom is better than jewels, and all that you may desire cannot compare with her.

12 I, Wisdom, dwell with prudence, and I find knowledge and discretion.

13 The fear of YHWH is hatred of evil. Pride and arrogance and the way of evil and perverted speech I hate.

14 I have counsel and sound wisdom — I have insight — I have strength.

15 By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just.

16 By me princes rule, and nobles, all who govern justly.

17 I love those who love me, and those who seek me diligently find me.

18 Riches and honor are with me, enduring wealth and righteousness.

19 My fruit is better than gold, even fine gold, and my yield than choice silver.

20 I walk in the way of righteousness, in the paths of justice,

21 Granting an inheritance to those who love me, and filling their treasuries.

22 YHWH possessed me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old.

23 Ages ago I was set up, at the first, before the beginning of the earth.

24 When there were no depths I was brought forth, when there were no springs abounding with water.

25 Before the mountains had been shaped, before the hills, I was brought forth,

26 Before he had made the earth with its fields, or the first of the dust of the world.

27 When he established the heavens, I was there — when he drew a circle on the face of the deep,

28 When he made firm the skies above, when he established the fountains of the deep,

29 When he assigned to the sea its limit, so that the waters might not transgress his command, when he marked out the foundations of the earth,

30 Then I was beside him, like a master workman, and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him always,

31 Rejoicing in his inhabited world and delighting in the children of man.

32 And now, O sons, listen to me — blessed are those who keep my ways.

33 Hear instruction and be wise, and do not neglect it.

34 Blessed is the one who listens to me, watching daily at my gates, waiting beside my doors.

35 For whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from YHWH,

36 But he who fails to find me injures himself — all who hate me love death.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 8. This chapter is the theological summit of the wisdom tradition — Wisdom speaking in the first person, declaring her presence at creation alongside YHWH as a master workman. Psalm 154 from the DSS Cave 11 Psalms Scroll draws directly on this chapter in its identification of Torah with personified Wisdom established before the world.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Verses 22-31 — the pre-creation existence of Wisdom beside YHWH as a master workman, rejoicing before him always — are read as the foundational declaration of Torah's eternal and cosmic status in the Beta Israel theological tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 8. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 22 — YHWH possessed me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old — is one of the most theologically significant verses in the entire Hebrew canon regarding the nature and origin of Wisdom.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 22-31 — the pre-creation monologue of Wisdom. She was possessed by YHWH before the beginning, set up before the earth existed, brought forth before the mountains were shaped. When YHWH drew the circle on the face of the deep — the same tehom of Bereshit 1:2 — Wisdom was there. When he assigned the sea its limit, Wisdom was there beside him as a master workman, daily his delight, rejoicing before him always. This is the most complete statement in the canonical Hebrew scriptures of Wisdom's cosmic and pre-temporal existence. Psalm 154 from the DSS develops this directly: she was established before the world and forever shall she endure. Wisdom is not created for humanity — humanity is created into a world where Wisdom already exists and already delights.

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 9 Wisdom Has Built Her House — The Fear of YHWH Is the Beginning of Wisdom — The Foolish Woman

1 Wisdom has built her house — she has hewn her seven pillars.

2 She has slaughtered her beasts — she has mixed her wine — she has also set her table.

3 She has sent out her young women to call from the highest places in the town:

4 Whoever is simple, let him turn in here. To him who lacks sense she says,

5 Come, eat of my bread and drink of the wine I have mixed.

6 Leave your simple ways, and live, and walk in the way of insight.

7 Whoever corrects a scoffer gets himself abuse, and he who reproves a wicked man incurs injury.

8 Do not reprove a scoffer, or he will hate you — reprove a wise man, and he will love you.

9 Give instruction to a wise man, and he will be still wiser — teach a righteous man, and he will increase in learning.

10 The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.

11 For by me your days will be multiplied, and years will be added to your life.

12 If you are wise, you are wise for yourself — if you scoff, you alone will bear it.

13 The foolish woman is loud — she is seductive and knows nothing.

14 She sits at the door of her house — she takes a seat on the highest places of the town,

15 Calling to those who pass by, who are going straight on their way:

16 Whoever is simple, let him turn in here. And to him who lacks sense she says,

17 Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant.

18 But he does not know that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 9. The chapter sets Wisdom's feast — seven pillars, prepared table, mixed wine — directly against the Foolish Woman's counterfeit invitation. Both cry out from the high places of the city. The contrast is the structural conclusion of the wisdom discourses of Chapters 1-9.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Wisdom's seven-pillared house is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the image of the covenant community built on the complete structure of YHWH's instruction — seven being the number of covenant completeness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 9. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 10 — the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding — echoes the opening declaration of Psalm 111:10 and the foundational axiom of 1:7, forming an inclusio around the entire wisdom discourse of Chapters 1-9.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant. The Foolish Woman offers the identical invitation as Wisdom — come, eat and drink — but the substance is counterfeit: stolen water instead of Wisdom's mixed wine, secret bread instead of Wisdom's prepared feast. The appeal is not to depravity but to the simple person's appetite for what appears to be the same thing. Verse 18 — but he does not know that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol. The repeated phrase from Chapter 7: he does not know. The danger of the Foolish Woman is not that she is obviously evil but that she appears to offer the same thing as Wisdom while leading in the opposite direction.

THE PROVERBS OF SHLOMO — CHAPTER 10 BEGINS

MISHLEI — CHAPTER 10 The Proverbs of Shlomo — A Wise Son — Righteousness and Wickedness Contrasted

1 The proverbs of Shlomo. A wise son makes a glad father, but a foolish son is a sorrow to his mother.

2 Treasures gained by wickedness do not profit, but righteousness delivers from death.

3 YHWH does not let the righteous go hungry, but he thwarts the craving of the wicked.

4 A slack hand causes poverty, but the hand of the diligent makes rich.

5 He who gathers in summer is a prudent son, but he who sleeps in harvest is a son who brings shame.

6 Blessings are on the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.

7 The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.

8 The wise of heart will receive commandments, but a babbling fool will come to ruin.

9 Whoever walks in integrity walks securely, but he who makes his ways crooked will be found out.

10 Whoever winks the eye causes trouble, and a babbling fool will come to ruin.

11 The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.

12 Hatred stirs up strife, but love covers all offenses.

13 On the lips of him who has understanding, wisdom is found, but a rod is for the back of him who lacks sense.

14 The wise lay up knowledge, but the mouth of a fool brings ruin near.

15 A rich man's wealth is his strong city — the poverty of the poor is their ruin.

16 The wage of the righteous leads to life — the gain of the wicked to sin.

17 Whoever heeds instruction is on the path to life, but he who rejects reproof leads others astray.

18 The one who conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever utters slander is a fool.

19 When words are many, transgression is not lacking, but whoever restrains his lips is prudent.

20 The tongue of the righteous is choice silver — the heart of the wicked is of little worth.

21 The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of sense.

22 The blessing of YHWH makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it.

23 Doing wrong is like a joke to a fool, but wisdom is pleasure to a man of understanding.

24 What the wicked dreads will come upon him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted.

25 When the tempest passes, the wicked is no more, but the righteous is established forever.

26 Like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is the sluggard to those who send him.

27 The fear of YHWH prolongs life, but the years of the wicked will be short.

28 The hope of the righteous brings joy, but the expectation of the wicked will perish.

29 The way of YHWH is a stronghold to the blameless, but destruction to evildoers.

30 The righteous will never be removed, but the wicked will not dwell in the land.

31 The mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom, but the perverse tongue will be cut off.

32 The lips of the righteous know what is acceptable, but the mouth of the wicked, what is perverse.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No direct DSS manuscript attestation for Mishlei Chapter 10. Chapter 10 opens the Shlomo proverb collection — individual two-line sayings in antithetical parallelism — that runs through Chapter 22:16. Each proverb pairs a righteous action or character with a wicked one, or wisdom with folly. The pattern is consistent and sustained.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. The collection of antithetical proverbs beginning in verse 1 is read as the practical distillation of the covenant wisdom tradition — each saying a compressed statement of covenant consequence.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants for Mishlei Chapter 10. The Masoretic text is the primary witness. Verse 12 — hatred stirs up strife, but love covers all offenses — is one of the most frequently cited proverbs in the later covenant tradition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — hatred stirs up strife, but love covers all offenses. The word love here is ahavah — the same covenant love of Devarim 6:5. The covering of offenses is not concealment or tolerance of evil — it is the active choice to maintain relationship over winning disputes. Verse 19 — when words are many, transgression is not lacking, but whoever restrains his lips is prudent. The connection between the quantity of speech and the quantity of sin is a consistent wisdom observation: more words, more error. The ability to be silent is itself a form of wisdom. Verse 22 — the blessing of YHWH makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it. The wealth that comes from YHWH's blessing is qualitatively different from wealth accumulated through wickedness: no sorrow is added to it. It does not cost the inner life.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 10
